title: Uniface Packing Codes
text: Uniface Packing Codes Packing codes are mappings between Uniface data types and DBMS storage formats. When writing a field, Uniface passes the data and a valid packing code to the DBMS connector, which uses the packing code to determine the appropriate storage format for storing the data in the target DBMS. Packing codes also determine how data is interfaced with 3GL routines. Uniface packing codes allow the designer to influence the mapping from Uniface data types to DBMS storage formats. They ensure that Uniface can guarantee the portability of Uniface applications. Packing codes can be defined, or redefined, on the model level and on the connector level (also called the internal level). Uniface automatically maps model-level packing codes to connector-level packing codes, so the connector only needs to be aware of the connector-level packing codes. When developing a DBMS connector, you must provide mappings from the connector-level packing codes to the DBMS storage formats. If the DBMS supports a storage format that is not covered by a Uniface connector-level packing code, this can be solved either by recognizing this limitation and never using that DBMS storage format, or by carrying out the required data conversion in the connector itself. The solution depends on the extent of the incompatibility. If Uniface supports a connector-level packing code that does not have a corresponding storage format in your DBMS, you can resolve this by mapping the packing code to one of the ASCII String packing codes, or by converting the data in the connector to map the packing code to an alternative storage format. Related Links Data Storage Field interface definitions, as defined in the Database Interface property, determine how data is stored in a specific DBMS storage format, and how Uniface deals with the data when it is retrieved. The definitions specify the Uniface data type and packing code, which are mapped by the DBMS connector to the DBMS data type, known as a storage format. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
